terms.KNM-WT 15000: The catalog number for the Turkana Boy skeleton — Kenya National Museum, West Turkana, specimen 15000. Discovered in 1984 at Nariokotome III on the west shore of Lake Turkana, it remains the most complete early Homo skeleton ever found, with 108 bone fragments recovered from approximately 1,500 cubic meters of screened sediment over five excavation seasons (1984–1989).
terms.Homo erectus: An archaic human species that lived from roughly 1.9 million to 110,000 years ago. Turkana Boy is classified as early Homo erectus (sometimes called Homo ergaster in Africa). This species was the first hominin with modern body proportions — long legs, narrow trunk, and a body clearly adapted for endurance locomotion in open landscapes.
terms.Nariokotome: The site on the west shore of Lake Turkana, Kenya, where the skeleton was found. Nariokotome III is the specific locality — an eroded slope where Kamoya Kimeu spotted a matchbox-sized cranial fragment on August 23, 1984. The sediments date to approximately 1.53 million years ago.
terms.Kamoya Kimeu: The Kenyan fossil hunter who discovered KNM-WT 15000 on August 23, 1984. Kimeu discovered more major hominin specimens than any other individual in history, including KNM-ER 1813 (Homo habilis), KNM-ER 3733 (Homo erectus), and KNM-WT 17000 (the 'Black Skull'). He worked with the Leakey family for over four decades and received the National Geographic Society's LaGorce Medal in 1985.
terms.Alan Walker: American paleoanthropologist (1938–2017) who served as senior anatomist on the Nariokotome project. He co-edited the definitive 1993 monograph with Richard Leakey and coordinated the technical analysis of the skeleton's body proportions — the long legs, narrow trunk, and heat-adapted form that anchor the 'first modern body' argument.
terms.Richard Leakey: Kenyan paleoanthropologist and conservationist (1944–2022) who led the five-season excavation at Nariokotome III. He co-edited the 1993 monograph with Alan Walker and wrote the primary narrative account of the discovery in Origins Reconsidered (1992).
terms.B. Holly Smith: Dental development specialist at the University of Michigan who established that Turkana Boy's dental age (7.6–8.8 years) was significantly younger than the 11–13 years suggested by his skeletal maturation. This discrepancy is a key scientific tension: every claim about adult height and brain capacity depends on which age model is adopted.
terms.perikymata: Microscopic growth lines on tooth enamel surfaces that form at regular intervals during development. Counting perikymata on Turkana Boy's teeth provides independent evidence that early Homo grew teeth — and by inference, matured generally — faster than modern humans, supporting a younger age at death.
terms.Acheulean: A stone tool tradition characterized by large, symmetrically shaped handaxes. The Acheulean dates to approximately 1.76 million years ago, predating Turkana Boy by about 230,000 years. This tool culture is associated with Homo erectus and represents a significant cognitive advance over earlier Oldowan tools.
terms.thermoregulation: The process by which organisms maintain internal temperature. Peter Wheeler's thermoregulation hypothesis proposes that Turkana Boy's tall, narrow body form was an adaptation for heat dissipation in open African savanna environments — maximizing surface area relative to body mass to radiate heat more efficiently.
terms.Lake Turkana: A large alkaline lake in the East African Rift Valley of northern Kenya. The Turkana Basin is one of the world's richest fossil-bearing regions for hominin evolution, yielding specimens spanning millions of years. The west shore, where Turkana Boy was found, has sediments dating from roughly 4 million to 1 million years ago.
terms.Homo ergaster: A taxonomic name sometimes used for the African population of early Homo erectus. Some paleoanthropologists classify Turkana Boy as Homo ergaster to distinguish African specimens from Asian Homo erectus (like Java Man and Peking Man). The distinction remains debated — the essay uses Homo erectus in the broad, inclusive sense.
terms.dental vs. skeletal age: The central scientific puzzle of the specimen. Turkana Boy's tooth eruption pattern suggests he died around age 8, but his bone development (using modern human growth charts) suggests 11–13. This mismatch reveals that early Homo matured faster than us — a hybrid developmental pattern, slower than apes but not yet as prolonged as modern human childhood.
